127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.

141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.

146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	\leanok
150	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
151	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
152	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
153	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
154	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
155	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
156	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
157	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
158	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
159	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
160	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
161	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
162	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
163	\end{proof}

165	\section{Projective dimension}
166	\label{sec:ab_pd}
167	
168	\begin{definition}
169	
170	\leanok
171	  [Projective dimension]
172	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
173	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
174	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
175	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
176	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
177	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
178	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
179	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
180	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
181	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
182	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
183	  \[
184	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
185	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
186	  \]
187	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
188	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
189	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
190	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
191	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
192	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
193	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
194	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
198	\begin{itemize}
199	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
200	    iff \(M\) is free);
201	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
202	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
203	\end{itemize}
204	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
205	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
206	
207	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
208	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
209	
210	\begin{lemma}
211	
212	\leanok
213	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
214	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
215	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
216	  
217	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
218	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
219	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
220	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
221	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
222	  %  \begin{enumerate}
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
225	  %  \item
226	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
227	  %  \item
228	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
229	  %  \end{enumerate}"
230	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
231	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
232	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
233	  \(R\)-modules. Then
234	  \begin{enumerate}
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
238	  \end{enumerate}
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
242	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
243	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
244	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
245	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
246	%  from Lemma REF."
247	\begin{proof}
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	\leanok
253	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
254	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
255	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
256	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
257	  \[
258	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
263	  \]
264	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
265	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
266	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
267	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
268	\end{proof}

365	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
366	  \]
367	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
368	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
369	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
370	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
371	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
372	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
373	  sequences
374	  \[
375	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
376	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
377	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
378	  \]
379	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
380	  yields the chain of inequalities
381	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
382	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
383	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
384	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
385	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
386	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
387	
388	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
389	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
390	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
391	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
392	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
393	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
394	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
395	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
396	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
397	  \[
398	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
399	  \]
400	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
401	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
402	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
403	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
404	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
405	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
406	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
407	  \[
408	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
409	  \]
410	  Substituting yields
411	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
412	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
413	\end{proof}

542	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and

544	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
545	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
546	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
547	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
548	\end{corollary}

550	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
551	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
552	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)

554	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF

558	  
559

561	\leanok
562	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
563	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
564	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
565	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
566	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
567	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
568	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
569	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
570	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
571	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
572	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.

575	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
576	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
577	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
578	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every

580	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
581	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
582	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
583	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
584	\end{proof}

586	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
587	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
588	
589	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
590	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
591	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
592	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
593	
594	\begin{enumerate}
595	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
596	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
597	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
598	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
599	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
600	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
601	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
602	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
603	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
604	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
605	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
606	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
607	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
608	\end{enumerate}
609	
610	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
611	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
612	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
613	Corollary~REF are formalized in
614	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
615	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.

  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}

The inductive step
\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula is gated, as of the current pinned
Mathlib snapshot, by exactly three absent substrate ingredients
(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth that is now in hand
(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
decomposition that the proof block of
\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folds into four bullets:
its purpose is to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps can be formalised
independently (and where the dependency graph permits, in parallel).

\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).

\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
input to'':
\begin{verbatim}
                gap (1)
       minimal-resolution carving

                  |
                  v
                gap (3)
        snake lemma on minimal
        resolution under x in m
                  |
                  v
   +--------------+--------------+
   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
                  ^
                  |
                gap (4)
       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
       cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one})
\end{verbatim}
Gap (4) is already formalised
(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.

\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
other gaps:
\begin{center}
  \begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|c|c|}
    \hline
    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\
    \hline
    (1) & Minimal-resolution carving             & 80--120   & 1--2
        & absent             & none \\
    (2) & Stacks 00MF (``what is exact'')        & 150--200  & 2--3
        & absent             & none \\
    (3) & Snake lemma on minimal resolution      & 80--120   & 1--2
        & absent             & gap (1) \\
    (4) & Depth drops by one                     & ---       & ---
        & \textbf{CLOSED}    & --- \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{center}
The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof. The gap
(3) estimate covers the snake-lemma application that converts a
minimal resolution of \(M\) into a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, conditional on gap (1).

\paragraph{Closure assembly.} Once gaps (1) and (3) and gap (2) are
all formalised axiom-clean, the body of
\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
(the \(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\) inductive step in
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}) closes
in an estimated 80--120 LOC of assembly. The assembly proceeds:
(a) invoke gap (2) on the start of a minimal finite free resolution
of \(M\) to extract a common \(M\)- and \(R\)-regular element
\(x \in \mathfrak m\); (b) invoke gap (1) to normalise the
minimal-resolution structure; (c) invoke gap (3) on the snake-lemma
diagram, obtaining a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\) over \(R/(x)\)
of length \(\text{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), so
\(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\);
(d) apply gap (4)
(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}) twice, once on \(R\) and once on
\(M\), to deduce
\(\text{depth}(R/(x)) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
\(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\); (e) close by the
inductive hypothesis on \((R/(x), M/xM)\), since
\(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = k+1\) but the induction is on
\(\text{depth}(M)\), which has strictly decreased. The arithmetic
\(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) - 1 = \text{depth}(R) - 1\)
rearranges to the stated formula.

\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget
recorded in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}: the refined window is somewhat
tighter on the upper end (5--8 vs.\ 6--12 sequential) and is
substantially tighter under parallel dispatch (3--5 parallel-best
vs.\ 6--12).
